Data Flow Diagram

Solid lines = encrypted flows · Dashed red lines = unencrypted or boundary-crossing · 🔒 / ⚠ indicate encryption status. Dashes drift to indicate active flows.

🚧 Untrusted-Input Boundary Crossings

Flows where untrusted (or less-trusted) input crosses into an internal trust zone. These are the highest-priority validation points in the system — every byte that enters here must be treated as hostile until proven otherwise.

🚧 Agent Gateway → Identity Provider / Okta MCP Critical

DMZ (public-facing) → Third-party / Untrusted services
Flow: Agent Gateway → Identity Provider / Okta MCP · Protocol: HTTPS · Auth: bearer · Encryption: ✅ encrypted · 4 threats on this flow
⚠ Validation requirements at the receiver:
  • Define an explicit allow-list schema (JSON Schema, OpenAPI, Protobuf) for accepted payloads — reject anything that doesn't match.
  • Validate every field's type, length, charset, and value range BEFORE business logic runs.
  • Canonicalize input (URL-decode, Unicode NFC, path-resolve) BEFORE validation to defeat bypass tricks.

All STRIDE Off Hidden (196)
Broken access control / missing authz check
Critical STRIDE · Elevation of Privilege · AI Detection & Response (api) CWE-269
▼
CVSS 3.1 8.3
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:L
CVSS 4.0 6.3
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:H/VI:H/VA:L/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=9, R=10, E=9, A=10, D=9 · 47/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component AI Detection & Response (api).

📝Description

Endpoints fail to verify the caller has permission for the action.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to AI Detection & Response as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to AI Detection & Response should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

🔗References

DREAD: D=9, R=8, E=9, A=10, D=9 · 45/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component AI Detection & Response (api).

📝Description

Attacker breaks out of a sandbox to gain host privileges.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to AI Detection & Response as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to AI Detection & Response should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

Stored data tampering via injection
Critical STRIDE · Tampering · Agent Evaluation (datastore) CWE-345
▼
CVSS 3.1 7.3
CVSS:3.1/AV:A/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.2
CVSS:4.0/AV:A/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:L/VI:H/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=10, R=10, E=9, A=7, D=9 · 45/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Agent Evaluation (datastore).

📝Description

SQL/NoSQL/command injection alters records or schema.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Agent Evaluation (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Agent Evaluation processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

🔗References

A03:2021 — Injection ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-345 — Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity ↗
Privilege transit across boundary: Core Agent Layer → Agent Evaluation
Critical STRIDE · Elevation of Privilege · Agent Evaluation (datastore) CWE-441 ⚡ cross-zone
▼
CVSS 3.1 8.9
CVSS:3.1/AV:A/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:L
CVSS 4.0 7.4
CVSS:4.0/AV:A/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:H/VI:H/VA:L/SC:H/SI:H/SA:L
DREAD: D=10, R=8, E=9, A=9, D=9 · 45/50
Boundary crossing: Application tier → Data tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Core Agent Layer → Agent Evaluation (label: *Core Agent Layer → Agent Evaluation*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Application tier into Data tier. The receiving component is Agent Evaluation (datastore).

📝Description

If the receiver in 'Data tier' acts on behalf of the caller, attackers compromising 'Application tier' may inherit the receiver's privileges (confused-deputy).

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a low-privilege component in zone `Application tier`.
  2. Issues requests to Agent Evaluation that get re-executed with the receiver's higher privileges (classic confused-deputy).
  3. Because authorization is checked once at request entry but not per-action against the originating caller, the attacker effectively becomes a privileged actor in `Data tier`.
  4. Attacker performs actions far beyond the original principal's permissions.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Use scoped, short-lived delegation tokens
When Agent Evaluation acts on behalf of a caller, attach a delegation token with the caller's identity, scope (action allow-list), and ≤ 5-min TTL.
preventive
Authorize per-action against the originating caller
Don't rely on ambient authority of the receiver. Each action checks: (a) the delegation token is valid, (b) the original caller has permission for this specific action, (c) the action is within scope.
detective
Audit each privilege transit
Log: original caller → delegating component → action performed. Make this queryable for incident response.

🔗References

A01:2021 — Broken Access Control ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-441 — Confused Deputy ↗

  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Agent Gateway should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective

Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

Boundary crossing: Internet → DMZ (public-facing)

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Users → Agent Gateway (label: *Users → Agent Gateway*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: session, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Internet into DMZ (public-facing). The receiving component is Agent Gateway (api).

📝Description

If the receiver in 'DMZ (public-facing)' acts on behalf of the caller, attackers compromising 'Internet' may inherit the receiver's privileges (confused-deputy).

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a low-privilege component in zone `Internet`.
  2. Issues requests to Agent Gateway that get re-executed with the receiver's higher privileges (classic confused-deputy).
  3. Because authorization is checked once at request entry but not per-action against the originating caller, the attacker effectively becomes a privileged actor in `DMZ (public-facing)`.
  4. Attacker performs actions far beyond the original principal's permissions.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Use scoped, short-lived delegation tokens
When Agent Gateway acts on behalf of a caller, attach a delegation token with the caller's identity, scope (action allow-list), and ≤ 5-min TTL.
preventive
Authorize per-action against the originating caller
Don't rely on ambient authority of the receiver. Each action checks: (a) the delegation token is valid, (b) the original caller has permission for this specific action, (c) the action is within scope.
detective
Audit each privilege transit
Log: original caller → delegating component → action performed. Make this queryable for incident response.

For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.

CVSS 4.0 6.3
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:H/VI:H/VA:L/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=9, R=10, E=9, A=10, D=9 · 47/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Agent Runtime / Orchestrator (api).

📝Description

Endpoints fail to verify the caller has permission for the action.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to Agent Runtime / Orchestrator as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Agent Runtime / Orchestrator should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

🔗References

A01:2021 — Broken Access Control ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-269 — Improper Privilege Management ↗
Container / process escape
Critical STRIDE · Elevation of Privilege · Agent Runtime / Orchestrator (api) CWE-269
▼
CVSS 3.1 8.3
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:L
CVSS 4.0 6.3
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:H/VI:H/VA:L/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=9, R=8, E=9, A=10, D=9 · 45/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Agent Runtime / Orchestrator (api).

📝Description

Attacker breaks out of a sandbox to gain host privileges.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to Agent Runtime / Orchestrator as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Agent Runtime / Orchestrator should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

🔗References

A01:2021 — Broken Access Control ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-269 — Improper Privilege Management ↗
Stored data tampering via injection
Critical STRIDE · Tampering · Agent Tools Catalog (datastore) CWE-345
▼
CVSS 3.1 7.3
CVSS:3.1/AV:A/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.2
CVSS:4.0/AV:A/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:L/VI:H/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=10, R=10, E=9, A=7, D=9 · 45/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Agent Tools Catalog (datastore).

📝Description

SQL/NoSQL/command injection alters records or schema.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Agent Tools Catalog (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Agent Tools Catalog processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

🔗References

A03:2021 — Injection ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-345 — Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity ↗
Privilege transit across boundary: Agent Runtime / Orchestrator → Agent Tools Catalog
Critical STRIDE · Elevation of Privilege · Agent Tools Catalog (datastore) CWE-441 ⚡ cross-zone
▼
CVSS 3.1 8.9
CVSS:3.1/AV:A/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:L
CVSS 4.0 7.4
CVSS:4.0/AV:A/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:H/VI:H/VA:L/SC:H/SI:H/SA:L
DREAD: D=10, R=8, E=9, A=9, D=9 · 45/50
Boundary crossing: Application tier → Data tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Agent Runtime / Orchestrator → Agent Tools Catalog (label: *Agent Runtime / Orchestrator → Agent Tools Catalog*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Application tier into Data tier. The receiving component is Agent Tools Catalog (datastore).

📝Description

If the receiver in 'Data tier' acts on behalf of the caller, attackers compromising 'Application tier' may inherit the receiver's privileges (confused-deputy).

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a low-privilege component in zone `Application tier`.
  2. Issues requests to Agent Tools Catalog that get re-executed with the receiver's higher privileges (classic confused-deputy).
  3. Because authorization is checked once at request entry but not per-action against the originating caller, the attacker effectively becomes a privileged actor in `Data tier`.
  4. Attacker performs actions far beyond the original principal's permissions.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Use scoped, short-lived delegation tokens
When Agent Tools Catalog acts on behalf of a caller, attach a delegation token with the caller's identity, scope (action allow-list), and ≤ 5-min TTL.
preventive
Authorize per-action against the originating caller
Don't rely on ambient authority of the receiver. Each action checks: (a) the delegation token is valid, (b) the original caller has permission for this specific action, (c) the action is within scope.
detective
Audit each privilege transit
Log: original caller → delegating component → action performed. Make this queryable for incident response.

🔗References

A01:2021 — Broken Access Control ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-441 — Confused Deputy ↗

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Core Agent Layer (api).

📝Description

Endpoints fail to verify the caller has permission for the action.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to Core Agent Layer as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Core Agent Layer should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

On the data flow Agent Gateway → Core Agent Layer (label: *Agent Gateway → Core Agent Layer*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from DMZ (public-facing) into Application tier. The receiving component is Core Agent Layer (api).

📝Description

If the receiver in 'Application tier' acts on behalf of the caller, attackers compromising 'DMZ (public-facing)' may inherit the receiver's privileges (confused-deputy).

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a low-privilege component in zone `DMZ (public-facing)`.
  2. Issues requests to Core Agent Layer that get re-executed with the receiver's higher privileges (classic confused-deputy).
  3. Because authorization is checked once at request entry but not per-action against the originating caller, the attacker effectively becomes a privileged actor in `Application tier`.
  4. Attacker performs actions far beyond the original principal's permissions.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Use scoped, short-lived delegation tokens
When Core Agent Layer acts on behalf of a caller, attach a delegation token with the caller's identity, scope (action allow-list), and ≤ 5-min TTL.
preventive
Authorize per-action against the originating caller
Don't rely on ambient authority of the receiver. Each action checks: (a) the delegation token is valid, (b) the original caller has permission for this specific action, (c) the action is within scope.
detective
Audit each privilege transit
Log: original caller → delegating component → action performed. Make this queryable for incident response.

CVSS 4.0 5.2
CVSS:4.0/AV:A/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:L/VI:H/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=10, R=10, E=9, A=7, D=9 · 45/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Long Term Memory (datastore).

📝Description

SQL/NoSQL/command injection alters records or schema.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Long Term Memory (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Long Term Memory processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

🔗References

A03:2021 — Injection ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-345 — Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity ↗
Privilege transit across boundary: Agent Runtime / Orchestrator → Long Term Memory
Critical STRIDE · Elevation of Privilege · Long Term Memory (datastore) CWE-441 ⚡ cross-zone
▼
CVSS 3.1 8.9
CVSS:3.1/AV:A/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:L
CVSS 4.0 7.4
CVSS:4.0/AV:A/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:H/VI:H/VA:L/SC:H/SI:H/SA:L
DREAD: D=10, R=8, E=9, A=9, D=9 · 45/50
Boundary crossing: Application tier → Data tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Agent Runtime / Orchestrator → Long Term Memory (label: *Agent Runtime / Orchestrator → Long Term Memory*, protocol: TCP, auth: credentials, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Application tier into Data tier. The receiving component is Long Term Memory (datastore).

📝Description

If the receiver in 'Data tier' acts on behalf of the caller, attackers compromising 'Application tier' may inherit the receiver's privileges (confused-deputy).

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a low-privilege component in zone `Application tier`.
  2. Issues requests to Long Term Memory that get re-executed with the receiver's higher privileges (classic confused-deputy).
  3. Because authorization is checked once at request entry but not per-action against the originating caller, the attacker effectively becomes a privileged actor in `Data tier`.
  4. Attacker performs actions far beyond the original principal's permissions.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Use scoped, short-lived delegation tokens
When Long Term Memory acts on behalf of a caller, attach a delegation token with the caller's identity, scope (action allow-list), and ≤ 5-min TTL.
preventive
Authorize per-action against the originating caller
Don't rely on ambient authority of the receiver. Each action checks: (a) the delegation token is valid, (b) the original caller has permission for this specific action, (c) the action is within scope.
detective
Audit each privilege transit
Log: original caller → delegating component → action performed. Make this queryable for incident response.

🔗References

A01:2021 — Broken Access Control ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-441 — Confused Deputy ↗
Broken access control / missing authz check
Critical STRIDE · Elevation of Privilege · MCP Server Hosting (api) CWE-269
▼
CVSS 3.1 8.3
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:L
CVSS 4.0 6.3
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:H/VI:H/VA:L/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=9, R=10, E=9, A=10, D=9 · 47/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component MCP Server Hosting (api).

📝Description

Endpoints fail to verify the caller has permission for the action.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to MCP Server Hosting as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to MCP Server Hosting should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

🔗References

A01:2021 — Broken Access Control ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-269 — Improper Privilege Management ↗
Container / process escape
Critical STRIDE · Elevation of Privilege · MCP Server Hosting (api) CWE-269
▼
CVSS 3.1 8.3
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:L
CVSS 4.0 6.3
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:H/VI:H/VA:L/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=9, R=8, E=9, A=10, D=9 · 45/50

📝Description

Attacker breaks out of a sandbox to gain host privileges.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to MCP Server Hosting as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to MCP Server Hosting should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

Boundary crossing: Application tier → Internet

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow MCP Server Hosting → MCP Server Targets (label: *MCP Server Hosting → MCP Server Targets*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: mtls, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Application tier into Internet. The receiving component is MCP Server Targets (external_entity).

📝Description

If the receiver in 'Internet' acts on behalf of the caller, attackers compromising 'Application tier' may inherit the receiver's privileges (confused-deputy).

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a low-privilege component in zone `Application tier`.
  2. Issues requests to MCP Server Targets that get re-executed with the receiver's higher privileges (classic confused-deputy).
  3. Because authorization is checked once at request entry but not per-action against the originating caller, the attacker effectively becomes a privileged actor in `Internet`.
  4. Attacker performs actions far beyond the original principal's permissions.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Use scoped, short-lived delegation tokens
When MCP Server Targets acts on behalf of a caller, attach a delegation token with the caller's identity, scope (action allow-list), and ≤ 5-min TTL.
preventive
Authorize per-action against the originating caller
Don't rely on ambient authority of the receiver. Each action checks: (a) the delegation token is valid, (b) the original caller has permission for this specific action, (c) the action is within scope.
detective
Audit each privilege transit
Log: original caller → delegating component → action performed. Make this queryable for incident response.

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Retrieval (RAG) (api).

📝Description

Endpoints fail to verify the caller has permission for the action.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to Retrieval (RAG) as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Retrieval (RAG) should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

🔗References

A01:2021 — Broken Access Control ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-269 — Improper Privilege Management ↗
Container / process escape
Critical STRIDE · Elevation of Privilege · Retrieval (RAG) (api) CWE-269
▼
CVSS 3.1 8.3
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:L
CVSS 4.0 6.3
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:H/VI:H/VA:L/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=9, R=8, E=9, A=10, D=9 · 45/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Retrieval (RAG) (api).

📝Description

Attacker breaks out of a sandbox to gain host privileges.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to Retrieval (RAG) as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Retrieval (RAG) should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

▼

DREAD: D=10, R=10, E=10, A=9, D=10 · 49/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component SailPoint Agent Identity (auth_service).

📝Description

Endpoints fail to verify the caller has permission for the action.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to SailPoint Agent Identity as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to SailPoint Agent Identity should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

🔗References

CVSS 4.0 6.3
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:H/VI:H/VA:L/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=10, R=8, E=10, A=9, D=10 · 47/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component SailPoint Agent Identity (auth_service).

📝Description

Attacker breaks out of a sandbox to gain host privileges.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to SailPoint Agent Identity as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to SailPoint Agent Identity should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

🔗References

A01:2021 — Broken Access Control ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-269 — Improper Privilege Management ↗
Privilege transit across boundary: Agent Gateway → SailPoint Agent Identity
Critical STRIDE · Elevation of Privilege · SailPoint Agent Identity (auth_service) CWE-441 ⚡ cross-zone
▼
CVSS 3.1 9.9
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:L
CVSS 4.0 7.4
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:H/VI:H/VA:L/SC:H/SI:H/SA:L
DREAD: D=10, R=8, E=10, A=10, D=10 · 48/50
Boundary crossing: DMZ (public-facing) → Application tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Agent Gateway → SailPoint Agent Identity (label: *Agent Gateway → SailPoint Agent Identity*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from DMZ (public-facing) into Application tier. The receiving component is SailPoint Agent Identity (auth_service).

📝Description

If the receiver in 'Application tier' acts on behalf of the caller, attackers compromising 'DMZ (public-facing)' may inherit the receiver's privileges (confused-deputy).

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a low-privilege component in zone `DMZ (public-facing)`.
  2. Issues requests to SailPoint Agent Identity that get re-executed with the receiver's higher privileges (classic confused-deputy).
  3. Because authorization is checked once at request entry but not per-action against the originating caller, the attacker effectively becomes a privileged actor in `Application tier`.
  4. Attacker performs actions far beyond the original principal's permissions.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Use scoped, short-lived delegation tokens
When SailPoint Agent Identity acts on behalf of a caller, attach a delegation token with the caller's identity, scope (action allow-list), and ≤ 5-min TTL.
preventive
Authorize per-action against the originating caller
Don't rely on ambient authority of the receiver. Each action checks: (a) the delegation token is valid, (b) the original caller has permission for this specific action, (c) the action is within scope.
detective
Audit each privilege transit
Log: original caller → delegating component → action performed. Make this queryable for incident response.

🔗References

A01:2021 — Broken Access Control ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-441 — Confused Deputy ↗
Stored data tampering via injection
Critical STRIDE · Tampering · Session Memory (datastore) CWE-345
▼
CVSS 3.1 7.3
CVSS:3.1/AV:A/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.2
CVSS:4.0/AV:A/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:L/VI:H/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=10, R=10, E=9, A=7, D=9 · 45/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Session Memory (datastore).

📝Description

SQL/NoSQL/command injection alters records or schema.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Session Memory (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Session Memory processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

🔗References

A03:2021 — Injection ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-345 — Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity ↗
Privilege transit across boundary: Agent Runtime / Orchestrator → Session Memory
Critical STRIDE · Elevation of Privilege · Session Memory (datastore) CWE-441 ⚡ cross-zone
▼
CVSS 3.1 8.9
CVSS:3.1/AV:A/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:L
CVSS 4.0 7.4
CVSS:4.0/AV:A/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:H/VI:H/VA:L/SC:H/SI:H/SA:L
DREAD: D=10, R=8, E=9, A=9, D=9 · 45/50
Boundary crossing: Application tier → Data tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Agent Runtime / Orchestrator → Session Memory (label: *Agent Runtime / Orchestrator → Session Memory*, protocol: TCP, auth: credentials, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Application tier into Data tier. The receiving component is Session Memory (datastore).

📝Description

If the receiver in 'Data tier' acts on behalf of the caller, attackers compromising 'Application tier' may inherit the receiver's privileges (confused-deputy).

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a low-privilege component in zone `Application tier`.
  2. Issues requests to Session Memory that get re-executed with the receiver's higher privileges (classic confused-deputy).
  3. Because authorization is checked once at request entry but not per-action against the originating caller, the attacker effectively becomes a privileged actor in `Data tier`.
  4. Attacker performs actions far beyond the original principal's permissions.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Use scoped, short-lived delegation tokens
When Session Memory acts on behalf of a caller, attach a delegation token with the caller's identity, scope (action allow-list), and ≤ 5-min TTL.
preventive
Authorize per-action against the originating caller
Don't rely on ambient authority of the receiver. Each action checks: (a) the delegation token is valid, (b) the original caller has permission for this specific action, (c) the action is within scope.
detective
Audit each privilege transit
Log: original caller → delegating component → action performed. Make this queryable for incident response.

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to Vector DB should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

🔗References

A01:2021 — Broken Access Control ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-269 — Improper Privilege Management ↗
Privilege transit across boundary: Retrieval (RAG) → Vector DB
Critical STRIDE · Elevation of Privilege · Vector DB (database) CWE-441 ⚡ cross-zone
▼
CVSS 3.1 8.9
CVSS:3.1/AV:A/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:L
CVSS 4.0 7.4
CVSS:4.0/AV:A/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:H/VI:H/VA:L/SC:H/SI:H/SA:L
DREAD: D=10, R=8, E=9, A=10, D=9 · 46/50
Boundary crossing: Application tier → Data tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Retrieval (RAG) → Vector DB (label: *Retrieval (RAG) → Vector DB*, protocol: TCP, auth: credentials, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Application tier into Data tier. The receiving component is Vector DB (database).

📝Description

If the receiver in 'Data tier' acts on behalf of the caller, attackers compromising 'Application tier' may inherit the receiver's privileges (confused-deputy).

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a low-privilege component in zone `Application tier`.
  2. Issues requests to Vector DB that get re-executed with the receiver's higher privileges (classic confused-deputy).
  3. Because authorization is checked once at request entry but not per-action against the originating caller, the attacker effectively becomes a privileged actor in `Data tier`.
  4. Attacker performs actions far beyond the original principal's permissions.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Use scoped, short-lived delegation tokens
When Vector DB acts on behalf of a caller, attach a delegation token with the caller's identity, scope (action allow-list), and ≤ 5-min TTL.
preventive
Authorize per-action against the originating caller
Don't rely on ambient authority of the receiver. Each action checks: (a) the delegation token is valid, (b) the original caller has permission for this specific action, (c) the action is within scope.
detective
Audit each privilege transit
Log: original caller → delegating component → action performed. Make this queryable for incident response.

🔗References

A01:2021 — Broken Access Control ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-441 — Confused Deputy ↗
Privilege transit across boundary: Agent Runtime / Orchestrator → Web Search
Critical STRIDE · Elevation of Privilege · Web Search (external_entity) CWE-441 ⚡ cross-zone
▼
CVSS 3.1 9.9
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:L
CVSS 4.0 7.4
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:H/VI:H/VA:L/SC:H/SI:H/SA:L
DREAD: D=9, R=8, E=10, A=9, D=10 · 46/50
Boundary crossing: Application tier → Internet

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Agent Runtime / Orchestrator → Web Search (label: *Agent Runtime / Orchestrator → Web Search*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: api_key, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Application tier into Internet. The receiving component is Web Search (external_entity).

📝Description

If the receiver in 'Internet' acts on behalf of the caller, attackers compromising 'Application tier' may inherit the receiver's privileges (confused-deputy).

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a low-privilege component in zone `Application tier`.
  2. Issues requests to Web Search that get re-executed with the receiver's higher privileges (classic confused-deputy).
  3. Because authorization is checked once at request entry but not per-action against the originating caller, the attacker effectively becomes a privileged actor in `Internet`.
  4. Attacker performs actions far beyond the original principal's permissions.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Use scoped, short-lived delegation tokens
When Web Search acts on behalf of a caller, attach a delegation token with the caller's identity, scope (action allow-list), and ≤ 5-min TTL.
preventive
Authorize per-action against the originating caller
Don't rely on ambient authority of the receiver. Each action checks: (a) the delegation token is valid, (b) the original caller has permission for this specific action, (c) the action is within scope.
detective
Audit each privilege transit
Log: original caller → delegating component → action performed. Make this queryable for incident response.

Boundary crossing: Application tier → Internet

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Core Agent Layer → Wiz AI Security Lens (label: *Core Agent Layer → Wiz AI Security Lens*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Application tier into Internet. The receiving component is Wiz AI Security Lens (external_entity).

📝Description

If the receiver in 'Internet' acts on behalf of the caller, attackers compromising 'Application tier' may inherit the receiver's privileges (confused-deputy).

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a low-privilege component in zone `Application tier`.
  2. Issues requests to Wiz AI Security Lens that get re-executed with the receiver's higher privileges (classic confused-deputy).
  3. Because authorization is checked once at request entry but not per-action against the originating caller, the attacker effectively becomes a privileged actor in `Internet`.
  4. Attacker performs actions far beyond the original principal's permissions.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Use scoped, short-lived delegation tokens
When Wiz AI Security Lens acts on behalf of a caller, attach a delegation token with the caller's identity, scope (action allow-list), and ≤ 5-min TTL.
preventive
Authorize per-action against the originating caller
Don't rely on ambient authority of the receiver. Each action checks: (a) the delegation token is valid, (b) the original caller has permission for this specific action, (c) the action is within scope.
detective
Audit each privilege transit
Log: original caller → delegating component → action performed. Make this queryable for incident response.

A01:2021 — Broken Access Control ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-441 — Confused Deputy ↗
Authentication bypass via credential stuffing
High STRIDE · Spoofing · AI Detection & Response (api) CWE-287
▼
CVSS 3.1 7.5
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N
CVSS 4.0 7.0
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:H/VI:N/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=7, A=8, D=7 · 35/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component AI Detection & Response (api).

📝Description

Attackers reuse leaked credentials to impersonate legitimate users.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker locates AI Detection & Response's identity claim mechanism (token format, header name, signing key handling).
  2. Forges or replays a token to assert another principal's identity.
  3. AI Detection & Response accepts the claim because verification is missing or weak (e.g., signature not checked, expired tokens accepted, no audience validation).
  4. Attacker acts as the spoofed principal — reading data, triggering workflows, or pivoting to internal services.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate token signatures with explicit algorithm allow-listing
Reject 'alg: none', reject HS256 when expecting RS256. Use a vetted JWT library; never hand-roll verification.
preventive
Enforce audience and issuer claims
Every token must declare which service it's intended for. Reject tokens whose audience doesn't match this component.
preventive
Bind tokens to a session or device
Use mTLS-bound tokens, DPoP, or token binding to prevent replay if a token is captured.

🔗References

A07:2021 — Identification and Authentication Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗
Session token hijacking
High STRIDE · Spoofing · AI Detection & Response (api) CWE-287

DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=7, A=8, D=7 · 35/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component AI Detection & Response (api).

📝Description

Attacker steals or predicts a session token to impersonate a user.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker locates AI Detection & Response's identity claim mechanism (token format, header name, signing key handling).
  2. Forges or replays a token to assert another principal's identity.
  3. AI Detection & Response accepts the claim because verification is missing or weak (e.g., signature not checked, expired tokens accepted, no audience validation).
  4. Attacker acts as the spoofed principal — reading data, triggering workflows, or pivoting to internal services.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate token signatures with explicit algorithm allow-listing
Reject 'alg: none', reject HS256 when expecting RS256. Use a vetted JWT library; never hand-roll verification.
preventive
Enforce audience and issuer claims
Every token must declare which service it's intended for. Reject tokens whose audience doesn't match this component.
preventive
Bind tokens to a session or device
Use mTLS-bound tokens, DPoP, or token binding to prevent replay if a token is captured.

API key / service identity spoofing
High STRIDE · Spoofing · AI Detection & Response (api) CWE-287

CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N

DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=7, A=8, D=7 · 35/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component AI Detection & Response (api).

📝Description

An attacker obtains or forges an API key to act as a trusted service.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker locates AI Detection & Response's identity claim mechanism (token format, header name, signing key handling).
  2. Forges or replays a token to assert another principal's identity.
  3. AI Detection & Response accepts the claim because verification is missing or weak (e.g., signature not checked, expired tokens accepted, no audience validation).
  4. Attacker acts as the spoofed principal — reading data, triggering workflows, or pivoting to internal services.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate token signatures with explicit algorithm allow-listing
Reject 'alg: none', reject HS256 when expecting RS256. Use a vetted JWT library; never hand-roll verification.
preventive
Enforce audience and issuer claims
Every token must declare which service it's intended for. Reject tokens whose audience doesn't match this component.
preventive
Bind tokens to a session or device
Use mTLS-bound tokens, DPoP, or token binding to prevent replay if a token is captured.

Log tampering
High STRIDE · Repudiation · AI Detection & Response (api) CWE-778
▼
CVSS 3.1 7.5
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:H/A:N
CVSS 4.0 7.0
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:N/VI:H/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=7, A=8, D=7 · 35/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component AI Detection & Response (api).

📝Description

An attacker with access modifies or deletes audit trails.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. User performs a sensitive action through AI Detection & Response (a transfer, a permission change, a deletion).
  2. Audit logs are absent, incomplete, or modifiable by the same principal who performed the action.
  3. User later denies having performed the action, or an attacker covers their tracks.
  4. Without tamper-evident logs, neither responsible party can be identified — leading to fraud, dispute, or regulatory exposure.

🛡How to mitigate

detective
Emit tamper-evident audit logs
Sign log entries (HMAC chain, hash-linked) so insertion or deletion is detectable. Forward to a separate, append-only system the action's principal cannot administer.
detective
Capture sufficient detail per audit event
Who (authenticated principal, not just session ID), what (specific action and target), when (UTC, monotonic-clock-corroborated), where (source IP, request ID), why (correlated to the upstream business event).
detective
Periodic log integrity verification
Schedule daily integrity checks on the audit log chain. Alert on any gap.

🔗References

A09:2021 — Security Logging and Monitoring Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-778 — Insufficient Logging ↗
Sensitive data exposure in transit
High STRIDE · Information Disclosure · AI Detection & Response (api) CWE-319
▼
CVSS 3.1 7.5
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N
CVSS 4.0 7.0
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:H/VI:N/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=7, A=8, D=8 · 36/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component AI Detection & Response (api).

📝Description

PII, secrets, or tokens transmitted over unencrypted channels.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches AI Detection & Response via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For api, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🔗References

A02:2021 — Cryptographic Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-319 — Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information ↗

  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

preventive

The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive

Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For api, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

Within component AI Detection & Response (api).

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches AI Detection & Response via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For api, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

Application-layer DDoS
High STRIDE · Denial of Service · AI Detection & Response (api) CWE-400
▼
CVSS 3.1 7.5
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:N/A:H
CVSS 4.0 7.0
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:N/VI:N/VA:H/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=7, A=8, D=7 · 35/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component AI Detection & Response (api).

📝Description

Attacker floods expensive endpoints (e.g., search, login).

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker identifies a costly operation in AI Detection & Response (regex matching, file generation, expensive query, large allocation).
  2. Issues many concurrent requests targeting that operation, or a single request with pathological input.
  3. AI Detection & Response's resources (CPU, memory, connections, disk) saturate or exhaust.
  4. Legitimate users can no longer reach AI Detection & Response; cascading failure may take down dependents.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply input limits at the edge
Maximum request size, body depth, array length, and string length. Reject early — before parsing or business logic runs.
preventive
Rate-limit per principal and per resource
Token bucket per authenticated user AND per costly endpoint. Adaptive throttling on saturation.
corrective
Set hard timeouts on outbound calls
No call (DB, downstream service, third-party API) should be able to hang the request handler indefinitely. Use circuit breakers and bulkheads.

🔗References

A05:2021 — Security Misconfiguration ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-400 — Uncontrolled Resource Consumption (DoS) ↗
Privilege escalation via mass assignment
High STRIDE · Elevation of Privilege · AI Detection & Response (api) CWE-269
▼
CVSS 3.1 8.3
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:L
CVSS 4.0 6.3
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:H/VI:H/VA:L/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=7, R=8, E=7, A=8, D=7 · 37/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component AI Detection & Response (api).

📝Description

User submits extra fields (e.g., role=admin) and they bind to the model.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker authenticates to AI Detection & Response as a low-privilege user, or reaches an unauthenticated entry point.
  2. Identifies an authorization gap — a function that doesn't re-check the caller's role, an IDOR-style URL, an admin endpoint with weak gating.
  3. Issues requests to that gap, gaining access to data or actions reserved for higher-privilege roles.
  4. Now operating with elevated privilege, attacker can pivot to other components, exfiltrate data, or persist access.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authorize on every action, not just at session start
Every privileged endpoint checks the current principal's roles/permissions for the specific resource being acted on. No 'logged in = allowed'.
preventive
Apply least-privilege to service identities
The IAM/role attached to AI Detection & Response should grant only the actions it actually performs. Audit IAM grants quarterly.
detective
Detect anomalous privilege use
Alert when a principal performs an action they have not performed in the trailing 90 days, or when admin-tier actions originate from non-admin networks.

🔗References

A01:2021 — Broken Access Control ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-269 — Improper Privilege Management ↗
Data-in-transit modification
High STRIDE · Tampering · Agent Evaluation (datastore) CWE-345
▼
CVSS 3.1 7.3
CVSS:3.1/AV:A/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.2
CVSS:4.0/AV:A/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:L/VI:H/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=8, R=6, E=7, A=5, D=7 · 33/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Agent Evaluation (datastore).

📝Description

Attacker on the network path alters request/response payloads.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches an input vector exposed by Agent Evaluation (request body, query string, message queue payload, persisted state).
  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Agent Evaluation processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.
preventive
Sign and verify integrity-critical messages
For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective
Use append-only / write-once stores for audit-critical data
Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

🔗References

A03:2021 — Injection ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-345 — Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity ↗

  2. Submits malformed input designed to alter the program's logic or stored data (injection, parameter tampering, deserialization gadgets).
  3. Agent Evaluation processes the input without strict validation, and the malicious change takes effect.
  4. Tampered data is persisted, executed, or relayed downstream — corrupting integrity beyond the original entry point.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Schema-validate and parameterize all inputs
JSON Schema / OpenAPI validation at API edges; parameterized queries / prepared statements for SQL; ORM-level validators on persisted models.

For commands or state mutations, attach an HMAC or signature so downstream consumers can detect tampering in transit or at rest.
detective

Where data must not be silently changed, write to an append-only log (e.g., immutable S3 bucket, CloudTrail-like store) and reconcile against the mutable copy.

For datastore, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

A02:2021 — Cryptographic Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Sensitive data exposure at rest
High STRIDE · Information Disclosure · Agent Evaluation (datastore) CWE-200
▼
CVSS 3.1 5.7
CVSS:3.1/AV:A/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.2
CVSS:4.0/AV:A/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:H/VI:N/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=8, R=6, E=7, A=5, D=8 · 34/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Agent Evaluation (datastore).

📝Description

Stored PII / secrets accessible without proper authorization.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Agent Evaluation via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For datastore, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

🔗References

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Agent Evaluation (datastore).

📝Description

User can access another user's resources by guessing IDs.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Agent Evaluation via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For datastore, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

Trust-boundary crossing without strong authn: Core Agent Layer → Agent Evaluation
High STRIDE · Spoofing · Agent Evaluation (datastore) CWE-287 ⚡ cross-zone
▼
CVSS 3.1 6.8
CVSS:3.1/AV:A/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:N/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.8
CVSS:4.0/AV:A/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:H/VI:N/VA:N/SC:H/SI:L/SA:N
DREAD: D=8, R=6, E=7, A=7, D=7 · 35/50
Boundary crossing: Application tier → Data tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Core Agent Layer → Agent Evaluation (label: *Core Agent Layer → Agent Evaluation*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Application tier into Data tier. The receiving component is Agent Evaluation (datastore).

📝Description

Flow 'Core Agent Layer → Agent Evaluation' crosses trust boundary 'Application tier' → 'Data tier'. Caller identity must be re-verified at the boundary; existing trust does not transit.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker positions themselves on the network path between source and destination, or compromises an upstream component in the source zone.
  2. Crafts requests with forged or replayed identity claims (cookies, tokens, IP-based trust).
  3. Agent Evaluation accepts the request because identity is not re-verified at the boundary, treating the upstream zone as trusted.
  4. Attacker can now perform actions as the impersonated principal across the boundary.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Re-authenticate at the boundary
Require a fresh, audience-bound credential at the receiver. Do not infer identity from network position, source IP, or upstream session.
preventive
Use mutual TLS or signed tokens with audience claims
For service-to-service calls into Agent Evaluation, terminate mTLS at the boundary and reject any caller without a valid client cert. For human-driven flows, use OAuth2 / OIDC with audience and issuer validation.
detective
Log every cross-boundary auth decision
Emit an authentication event (success and failure) tagged with both source and destination zone. Forward to SIEM with retention ≥ 90 days.

🔗References

A07:2021 — Identification and Authentication Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗
Cross-boundary input not validated: Core Agent Layer → Agent Evaluation
High STRIDE · Tampering · Agent Evaluation (datastore) CWE-20 ⚡ cross-zone
▼
CVSS 3.1 8.7
CVSS:3.1/AV:A/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.8
CVSS:4.0/AV:A/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:L/VI:H/VA:N/SC:L/SI:H/SA:N
DREAD: D=8, R=6, E=7, A=7, D=7 · 35/50
Boundary crossing: Application tier → Data tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Core Agent Layer → Agent Evaluation (label: *Core Agent Layer → Agent Evaluation*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Application tier into Data tier. The receiving component is Agent Evaluation (datastore).

📝Description

Data crossing the trust boundary into 'Data tier' must be treated as untrusted, even if the source is internal. Implicit trust is the most common cause of injection / SSRF / deserialization bugs.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker compromises a less-trusted upstream zone or directly sends malformed input to Agent Evaluation.
  2. Sends payloads with unexpected types, lengths, or special characters (e.g. SQL meta-chars, NUL bytes, control sequences).
  3. Agent Evaluation processes the input under the assumption that the upstream zone has already validated it — but the boundary changes the security context.
  4. Injection, deserialization flaws, or buffer issues trigger; attacker pivots into Agent Evaluation or the zone behind it.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate against an allow-list schema at the boundary
Define an explicit schema (JSON Schema, Protobuf, OpenAPI) for every accepted message at this boundary. Reject anything that doesn't match — type, length, charset, enum values, nested depth.
preventive
Canonicalize before validation
Decode URL-encoding, Unicode normalization (NFC), trim whitespace, and resolve relative paths before validating. Avoid double-decoding bypasses.
preventive
Apply context-specific output encoding
Whatever Agent Evaluation does with the input — SQL: parameterized queries, HTML: contextual escaping, OS commands: avoid shell, use exec arrays, LDAP: escape filter chars.

🔗References

A03:2021 — Injection ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-20 — Improper Input Validation ↗
Cross-boundary data exposure risk: Core Agent Layer → Agent Evaluation
High STRIDE · Information Disclosure · Agent Evaluation (datastore) CWE-200 ⚡ cross-zone
▼
CVSS 3.1 6.8
CVSS:3.1/AV:A/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:N/A:N
CVSS 4.0 5.8
CVSS:4.0/AV:A/AC:L/AT:N/PR:L/UI:N/VC:H/VI:N/VA:N/SC:H/SI:L/SA:N
DREAD: D=8, R=6, E=7, A=7, D=8 · 36/50
Boundary crossing: Application tier → Data tier

📍Where the threat exists

On the data flow Core Agent Layer → Agent Evaluation (label: *Core Agent Layer → Agent Evaluation*, protocol: HTTPS, auth: bearer, encrypted: yes), crossing the trust boundary from Application tier into Data tier. The receiving component is Agent Evaluation (datastore).

📝Description

Information leaving 'Application tier' into 'Data tier' may include data the receiving zone is not authorized to see. Cross-boundary egress is a common data-leak surface.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker observes traffic leaving the source zone, or compromises a component in the destination zone.
  2. The destination zone receives more data than it strictly needs (over-fetching, verbose error responses, full record dumps).
  3. Attacker harvests sensitive fields (PII, secrets, internal IDs) that should never have left the source zone.
  4. Data is exfiltrated, sold, or used to plan a deeper attack on the originating zone.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Enforce minimum-data-needed at the egress point
Whitelist exactly which fields leave the source zone. Strip everything else server-side before serialization.
preventive
Tokenize or redact sensitive fields
Replace PII / PCI / PHI fields with reversible tokens or one-way hashes when the destination zone doesn't need the cleartext value.
detective
Log cross-boundary data flows for review
Sample-log the field set crossing this boundary (not the values) so DLP and privacy reviews can audit what's egressing.

🔗References

A02:2021 — Cryptographic Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-200 — Exposure of Sensitive Information ↗
Authentication bypass via credential stuffing
High STRIDE · Spoofing · Agent Gateway (api) CWE-287
▼
CVSS 3.1 7.5
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N
CVSS 4.0 7.0
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:H/VI:N/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=9, A=9, D=9 · 40/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Agent Gateway (api).

📝Description

Attackers reuse leaked credentials to impersonate legitimate users.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker locates Agent Gateway's identity claim mechanism (token format, header name, signing key handling).
  2. Forges or replays a token to assert another principal's identity.
  3. Agent Gateway accepts the claim because verification is missing or weak (e.g., signature not checked, expired tokens accepted, no audience validation).
  4. Attacker acts as the spoofed principal — reading data, triggering workflows, or pivoting to internal services.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate token signatures with explicit algorithm allow-listing
Reject 'alg: none', reject HS256 when expecting RS256. Use a vetted JWT library; never hand-roll verification.
preventive
Enforce audience and issuer claims
Every token must declare which service it's intended for. Reject tokens whose audience doesn't match this component.
preventive
Bind tokens to a session or device
Use mTLS-bound tokens, DPoP, or token binding to prevent replay if a token is captured.

🔗References

A07:2021 — Identification and Authentication Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗
Session token hijacking
High STRIDE · Spoofing · Agent Gateway (api) CWE-287
▼
CVSS 3.1 7.5
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N
CVSS 4.0 7.0
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:H/VI:N/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=9, A=9, D=9 · 40/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Agent Gateway (api).

📝Description

Attacker steals or predicts a session token to impersonate a user.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker locates Agent Gateway's identity claim mechanism (token format, header name, signing key handling).
  2. Forges or replays a token to assert another principal's identity.
  3. Agent Gateway accepts the claim because verification is missing or weak (e.g., signature not checked, expired tokens accepted, no audience validation).
  4. Attacker acts as the spoofed principal — reading data, triggering workflows, or pivoting to internal services.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate token signatures with explicit algorithm allow-listing
Reject 'alg: none', reject HS256 when expecting RS256. Use a vetted JWT library; never hand-roll verification.
preventive
Enforce audience and issuer claims
Every token must declare which service it's intended for. Reject tokens whose audience doesn't match this component.
preventive
Bind tokens to a session or device
Use mTLS-bound tokens, DPoP, or token binding to prevent replay if a token is captured.

DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=9, A=9, D=9 · 40/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Agent Gateway (api).

📝Description

An attacker obtains or forges an API key to act as a trusted service.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker locates Agent Gateway's identity claim mechanism (token format, header name, signing key handling).
  2. Forges or replays a token to assert another principal's identity.
  3. Agent Gateway accepts the claim because verification is missing or weak (e.g., signature not checked, expired tokens accepted, no audience validation).
  4. Attacker acts as the spoofed principal — reading data, triggering workflows, or pivoting to internal services.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Validate token signatures with explicit algorithm allow-listing
Reject 'alg: none', reject HS256 when expecting RS256. Use a vetted JWT library; never hand-roll verification.
preventive
Enforce audience and issuer claims
Every token must declare which service it's intended for. Reject tokens whose audience doesn't match this component.
preventive
Bind tokens to a session or device
Use mTLS-bound tokens, DPoP, or token binding to prevent replay if a token is captured.

A07:2021 — Identification and Authentication Failures ↗STRIDE reference ↗CWE-287 — Improper Authentication ↗
Log tampering
High STRIDE · Repudiation · Agent Gateway (api) CWE-778
▼
CVSS 3.1 7.5
CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:H/A:N
CVSS 4.0 7.0
CVSS:4.0/AV:N/AC:L/AT:N/PR:N/UI:N/VC:N/VI:H/VA:N/SC:N/SI:N/SA:N
DREAD: D=7, R=6, E=9, A=9, D=9 · 40/50

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Agent Gateway (api).

📝Description

An attacker with access modifies or deletes audit trails.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. User performs a sensitive action through Agent Gateway (a transfer, a permission change, a deletion).
  2. Audit logs are absent, incomplete, or modifiable by the same principal who performed the action.
  3. User later denies having performed the action, or an attacker covers their tracks.
  4. Without tamper-evident logs, neither responsible party can be identified — leading to fraud, dispute, or regulatory exposure.

🛡How to mitigate

detective
Emit tamper-evident audit logs
Sign log entries (HMAC chain, hash-linked) so insertion or deletion is detectable. Forward to a separate, append-only system the action's principal cannot administer.
detective
Capture sufficient detail per audit event
Who (authenticated principal, not just session ID), what (specific action and target), when (UTC, monotonic-clock-corroborated), where (source IP, request ID), why (correlated to the upstream business event).
detective
Periodic log integrity verification
Schedule daily integrity checks on the audit log chain. Alert on any gap.

🔗References

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Agent Gateway (api).

📝Description

PII, secrets, or tokens transmitted over unencrypted channels.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Agent Gateway via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
  4. Uses the harvested information to plan a targeted attack, reset accounts, or commit fraud.

🛡How to mitigate

preventive
Apply field-level access control on responses
The data layer enforces what the calling principal is allowed to see. Don't rely on the UI to hide fields.
preventive
Strip debug detail from error responses
Production responses return a stable error code and a correlation ID. Stack traces, SQL errors, and internal IDs go to logs only.
preventive
Encrypt data at rest with key separation
For api, enable storage-level encryption with a CMK distinct from the encryption-at-rest key for adjacent stores. Rotate annually.

📍Where the threat exists

Within component Agent Gateway (api).

📝Description

User can access another user's resources by guessing IDs.

⚔️Attack scenario

  1. Attacker reaches Agent Gateway via a legitimate or guessable channel.
  2. Triggers an error, edge case, or verbose endpoint that returns more information than necessary (stack traces, internal IDs, debug data, full PII fields).
  3. Aggregates leaked information from multiple requests to build a profile of the system or its users.
